exemplar.title: How and why does discharge increase along the long profile of River Kall?

detailedExemplarData.title: How and why does discharge increase along the long profile of River Kall?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is precise, measurable and explicitly linked to the Bradshaw model, allowing hypothesis formation.
Specific location (River Kall in North-Rhine Westphalia) with feasibility for primary data collection is clearly justified.
Two scaled maps with annotated river course follow cartographic conventions broadly, aiding spatial understanding.
weaknesses: Map lacks a scale bar and north arrow, reducing full cartographic rigor.
Geographic context could be strengthened with precise coordinates or grid references.
Citation for the Bradshaw model is vague and should include full source details.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All strands meet the topâband descriptors: a clear geographic question with theoretical linkage, precise and feasible location, and appropriate mapping. Minor lapses in cartographic conventions and citation prevent further improvement but do not detract from a fullâband performance, meriting 3/3.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods for width, depth and velocity are described stepâbyâstep and linked to the discharge formula.
Primary data collection techniques are correctly used, yielding a dataset from 21 sites.
weaknesses: Sampling interval and site spacing rationale are not justified, weakening design reliability.
Units for dependent variables are inconsistently reported, affecting clarity.
Control variable protocol and use of an orange as float introduce uncertainties and reduce precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The methodology is clearly described and relevant to the research question, and data collection is correctly implemented. However, lack of sampling justification, unit inconsistencies and methodological imprecision limit the reliability of results, aligning with the midâband descriptor and warranting 2/3.

strengths: Collected data (width, depth, velocity) are directly relevant to the question, enabling meaningful analysis.
Presentation methods (tables, scatter graph with trend line and Pearsonâs r) are appropriate and clear.
weaknesses: Occasional unit errors (cm vs. m) and unclear axis labelling reduce clarity.
Only one graphical technique is used; additional presentation methods could strengthen interpretation.
Some discharge values appear anomalous, suggesting calculation or dataâentry issues.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data relevance and sufficiency are solid, and presentation techniques are appropriate. Occasional convention lapses and limited diversity of methods place this work in the 3-4 band, justifying 4/6.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Correct use of Pearsonâs correlation formula demonstrates sound statistical technique.
Key trends and anomalies are identified and linked to the Bradshaw model.
All major components of analysis (scatter, correlation, model discussion) are present.
weaknesses: No significance testing (pâvalue) or regression equation (RÂ²) is provided to quantify model fit.
Theoretical linkage to specific Bradshaw variables lacks depth.
Some evidence gaps remain (e.g., no error propagation, limited variability measures).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis demonstrates appropriate descriptive and statistical techniques, and links data to theory. Gaps in statistical rigor and incomplete exploration of processes limit the analysis to the midâband level (4/8).

strengths: Conclusion clearly restates the moderate positive correlation and directly addresses the research hypothesis.
Conclusion is fully supported by the analysis and data presented.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion meets the fullâband descriptor by providing a clear, explicitly supported summary of findings, meriting the maximum 2/2.

strengths: Strengths and weaknesses of the investigation are outlined with relevant explanations.
Improvement suggestions (e.g., repeated measurements, electronic instruments) are appropriate and briefly justified.
weaknesses: Evaluation lacks comprehensive depth; key aspects like statistical validity and mapping accuracy are not fully explored.
Improvement recommendations are not detailed with specific expected impacts.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation identifies relevant strengths, weaknesses and improvements but remains superficial in its critical depth and justification of impact. This aligns with the middle band and justifies 2/3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two scaled maps are provided (continental and catchment scale) with an annotated river course and coordinates. Cartographic conventions (title, legend implied by colour, north orientation) are broadly followed, fulfilling the requirements of the highest band.

The research question is clearly geographic, focuses on river discharge, and is explicitly linked to the Bradshaw model, allowing for hypothesis formulation. The wording is precise and measurable, meeting all elements of the top-band descriptor.

Tables, a scatter graph with trend line and Pearsonâs r are appropriate. Nevertheless, only one graphical technique is employed and axes are logarithmic-looking without clarification. These factors place presentation as âappropriateâ rather than âmost effectiveâ.

Techniques (tape, pole, float) are correctly used and yield a dataset from 21 sites; however, single readings at each site, irregular spacing and obvious unit inconsistencies reduce reliability. This matches the descriptor of correct but minimal quality primary data.

The student calculates discharge and Pearsonâs correlation coefficient, employing both descriptive (scatter) and one statistical technique. No confidence testing or regression diagnostics are attempted, situating the work in the 3-4 band.

Trends and one anomaly are identified and linked to tributary input and Bradshaw model theory, but discussion is brief and lacks depth on hydraulic radius or catchment processes. This aligns with âtrends and anomalies identified and linkedâ of the 3-4 band.

The student specifies the River Kall, situates it within North-Rhine Westphalia, explains why it is suitable for primary data collection (proximity, safety, accessibility) and references its geological setting. This level of locational detail fully satisfies the descriptor for precise location and feasibility.

Strengths and weaknesses are outlined with explanation, but evaluation is not fully comprehensiveâsampling design, statistical validity and mapping accuracy are not critically explored. This depth corresponds to the middle band.

Most collected data (width, depth, velocity) is directly relevant to answering the question, yet some values (e.g., 400 m width) suggest error, meaning relevance is high but sufficiency partially compromised. This fits the 3-4 band, lower end.
A clear, explicit conclusion restates the moderate positive correlation and references the hypothesis; it is directly supported by the analysis provided, meeting the full-support criterion of band 2.

All required components of analysis are present but limited: only one variability measure, no error propagation, and acknowledged data issues remain unresolved. The analysis therefore has âsome gaps in evidenceâ, fitting the 3-4 band.

Methods for width, depth and velocity are described step-by-step and linked to the discharge formula, but explanations remain brief and omit justification of sample size or statistical design. Alignment with theory is present but not comprehensive, fitting the middle band.

Titles and units are generally included, but there are frequent convention lapses: width appears in cm yet labelled âmâ; some tables omit units. Such âoccasional errorsâ correspond to the 3-4 mark descriptor.

Improvement suggestions are relevant (e.g., repeated measurements, equal spacing, electronic instruments) and briefly justified, yet impact on results is only superficially discussed, matching the descriptor for band 2.

annotations.comment: The research question is precise, measurable and explicitly linked to the Bradshaw model, allowing hypothesis formation and clear geographic focus.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Justification discusses theory broadly but lacks specific objectives and local context details; include explicit aims and how discharge relates to local human use or management.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Geographical context description lacks precise coordinates or scale context; include lat/long or grid references to improve locational specificity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The map lacks a scale bar and north arrow; ensure cartographic conventions are fully applied to improve spatial clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Citation for the Bradshaw model is vague (âUnknown, 2017â). Use a precise source with author and publication details to strengthen academic rigor.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Alternate hypothesis could be more precise by stating expected increase rate or pattern of discharge relative to distance downstream.
Description of the independent variable states measurement tool but omits sampling interval justification; explain site spacing rationale.
Dependent variables are listed without specifying units or measurement precision; include units (m, mÂ³/s) and resolution.
Control variable protocol relies on ad hoc obstacle removal in deep water; propose alternative controls for flow disturbance.
Methodology states groups of three but omits justification of sample size and spatial interval; explain why 21 sites and spacing were chosen.
Calculation paragraph is conflated with materials list; separate calculation steps and list materials distinctly.
Materials list omits devices used for gradient or hydraulic radius; include clinometer and profiling tools for full discharge analysis.
Depth measurement method is clear but should reference cross-sectional area calculation (Area = width Ã mean depth).
Using an orange for velocity introduces large uncertainties; consider a standardized float method or flow meter to improve precision.
Sample sites are irregularly spaced, which can distort trend analysis; standardize inter-site distances for consistent longitudinal profiling.
The uncertainty table lists formulas but does not compute actual percentage uncertainties; include numerical values to quantify error.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Raw data table shows widths in metres but initial measurements were in cm; clarify conversion and correct unit labels.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Table caption omits sample size (n = 21) and measurement units; include these in the caption for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Processed discharge table is titled as a section header rather than a figure/table caption; reformat to proper caption style.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Some discharge values appear anomalously high (e.g., 10,476 mÂ³/s at 14,364 m); verify calculations and ensure realistic ranges.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The Pearson formula is correctly presented and appropriate for measuring linear association.
The correlation coefficient lacks significance testing (p-value or comparison to critical r); include a statistical test to support inference.
Graph 1 presents a trendline but omits the regression equation and RÂ²; include these to quantify model fit.
Analysis notes moderate positive correlation but does not link specific Bradshaw model variables (e.g., width, depth) to observed trends; deepen theoretical linkage.
Comparison to literature summarises the Bradshaw model but omits quantitative predictions; cite specific model curves for direct comparison.
Conclusion clearly restates findings and is fully supported by analysis, demonstrating how hypothesis was confirmed.
Strengths table incorrectly refers to a negative correlation; the correlation is positive. Correct this to align with analysis.
Explanation of axis distortion confuses y-axis representation; the graph uses distance on x-axisâreview graph interpretation.
Improvement suggestions mention electronic devices but lack specifics; identify instruments (e.g., ultrasonic flow meter) and expected gains in precision.
Works cited entries are inconsistent in format and missing access dates; adopt a single citation style (e.g., APA) and include full details.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
